content.full_text:
State of California

• Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Meeting Participants                    Date : May 26, 1978


                                                            m:48-242
From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Alan Herndon

Subject: Contributions To The CMA Fuel Fund

Hughes Aircraft Company has inquired whether their contributions
to the California Manufacturers Association (CMA) Fuel Fund are
includable as payments to influence legislative or administrative
action for purposes of Section 86108(b).  The last paragraph on
Page 2 of the May 1978 fact sheet published by the CMA, a copy
of which is attached, indicates that the payments to the Fuel
Fund by the members are not includable.  I believe this statement
is incorrect.

Regulation 18618(b) states in part "Dues or similar payments made
by any person for membership in a bona fide association... need
not be included for the purpose of determining whether a person
must file reports pursuant to Government Code Section 86108(b)."
In opinion 75-169, the Commission concluded that payments by
members of the Fire Safety Coalition of California were not "Dues
or similar payments for membership" because the payments were
made voluntarily, free from any agreements or fee schedules.  The
facts in the present situation seem to be very similar to those
in Opinion 75-169, i.e. the payments are voluntary and are not
required to be made pursuant to a specific fee schedule.

Do you agree with my analysis of this situation?  Should we
contact the CMA about this?

RESCALTION:

AN ADVICE LETTER WAS SENT TO CMA ON 4/1/76
BY NAN HAMILTON STATING THAT CONTRIBUTIONS TO
THE FUEL FUND ARE NOT REPORTABLE BY THE MEMBER
MAKING THE PAYMENTS. IT WAS DECIDED THAT SINCE
THIS IS A CLOSE QUESTION THAT HAS BEEN PREVIOUSLY
BE DISCUSSED, THE PRIOR ADVICE IS STILL APPLICABLE.

                                        A.Herndon
                                        6/13/78

State of California

Memorandum

To     : Opinion Request Meeting Participants                     Date  : May 26, 1978


From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Alan Herndon

Subject: Contributions To The CMA Fuel Fund

     Hughes Aircraft Company has inquired whether their contributions
to the California Manufacturers Association (CMA) Fuel Fund are
includable as payments to influence legislative or administrative
action for purposes of Section 86108(b). The last paragraph on
Page 2 of the May 1978 fact sheet published by the CMA, a copy
of which is attached, indicates that the payments to the Fuel
Fund by the members are not includable. I believe this statement
is incorrect.

     Regulation 18618(b) states in part "Dues or similar payments made
by any person for membership in a bona fide association need
not be included for the purpose of determining whether a person
must file reports pursuant to Government Code Section 86108(b)."
In Opinion 75-169, the Commission concluded that payments by
members of the Fire Safety Coalition of California were not "Dues
or similar payments for membership" because the payments were
made voluntarily, free from any agreements or fee schedules. The
facts in the present situation seem to be very similar to those
in Opinion 75-169, i.e. the payments are voluntary and are not
required to be made pursuant to a specific fee schedule.

     Do you agree with my analysis of this situation?  Should we
contact the CMA about this?

CALIFORNIA MANUFACTURERS ASSOCIATION
923 12th Street    P.O. Box 1133 Sacramento, California 95805    916/441-5420

'78

May 1978

The "Hows" and "Whys" of
the Fuel Fund

California Manufacturers Association

successful in meeting necessary costs when major cases created special added expenses in this field. It is hoped that this need would not only a few years and could then be dispensed with.

HOW CAN A COMPANY HELP?

By contribution to the Fuel Fund. By participating in the Energy Committee activity. By sharing all the issues and by providing testimony on how specific rate proposals will affect your business. By discussing the matter with business associates and suggesting that they too get involved. By following the Sacramento Report for news on the issue and for notices of meetings.

IS THE EFFORT WORTH THE EXPENSES?

Yes. It is usually not possible and never prudent to place a precise value upon our rate cost participation, but any objective look at what likely would happen without it, will show the value. We are facing requests for increases of up to $300 million, with pressures for the greater part of this increase to be paid by industry.

ISN'T IT ALREADY TOO LATE?

No. In 1977, in the face of a PUC proposal to give all refunds to residential users, we helped secure passage of a bill that mandated refunds be made to all other customers. We think that this show of legislative concern and support in that face of a proposal that was very amenable to demographic carpets—is a sign that unfair rate proposals are a growing less politically attractive. We must state on effective cost minimum subsidy to small users. PUC decisions are subject to review by the California Supreme Court by an accelerated process whereby reasonably quick judicial hearing may be granted. Whenever the legal situation is appropriate, and the stakes high, we intend to go to the supreme Court. In addition, the medical views of the newest members of the PUC have not been subjected to the full body of evidence and argument on these matters. We feel that the evidence and reasoning overwhelmingly support rate structures based upon the cost of providing the service. Therefore, we feel that proper presentation of the evidence will plant the thrust of the radical rate proposals. We must now engage in a toughen effort to keep before the PUC, the legislature, and the public, the drawbacks of grant deviation from the actual cost sequence as a basis for utility rates. The first adverse economic impact of these new rate structures upon others is now occurring. The price of natural gas to large users is significantly higher than that of equivalent

substitute fuels. So they are shifting to those alternate fuels. Since the gas utilities had depended upon inordinate profits from large users to make up losses on sales to the residential users, the gas utilities are now facing a drastic profit pinch. Air Resources Board action to mandate taking of gas (at any price) has been suggested, and thus far, not taken.

DOES FUEL FUND ACTION OPPOSE UTILITY PROFITS?

No. A fundamental tenet of our policy for the past thirty years is that we support the need of the utility for a fair rate of return. We have never taken part in argument upon this matter, always stressing that our most important issue is how the costs are to be divided among customers. once the PUC has determined how large the total, including profit, should be.

WHAT ARE THE MECHANICS OF FUEL FUND PARTICIPATION?

Check your 1976 electric and natural gas bills and divide by 2000. Send a check for at least this amount to the Fuel Fund, California Manufacturers Association, P.O. Box 1139, Sacramento, 95805. If you wish an invoice, let us know the amount and we will send one. If your budgetary processes will not allow quick action, send us a pledge, so we can know our available resources.

WHAT IS THE TAX STATUS OF FUEL FUND CONTRIBUTIONS?

Fuel fund contributions are used entirely for professional services, legal and engineering, and the related expenses, such as travel, secretarial, mailing, etc., for the purpose of advocacy on matters directly related to your business costs. They are business expenses in every sense of the word and accepted as such by federal and state authorities. They are not charitable or political contributions.

HOW DOES PROPOSITION 9 AFFECT FUEL FUND CONTRIBUTIONS?

Under a recent ruling, any person contributing to the Fuel Fund need make no report whatever in connection with the Fair Political Practices Act, usually referred to as Prop. 9. Your Association must report the contributions it receives and how they are dispersed, but there is no reporting as those who contribute.
